symbols: lance or spear — the holy lance that pierced Christ's side, an instrument of the Passion
angel bearing the lance — the heavenly guardianship of sacred relics and Passion instruments
ornate embroidered robe — the glory and dignity of the angelic order
church buildings in background — the earthly Church protected by angelic guardians
wings — the celestial nature and swift service of angels
description: A stained-glass window panel depicting an angel with golden wings wearing an elaborate robe decorated with red and gold embroidered patterns. The angel holds a tall lance or spear upright. Long flowing hair falls over the shoulders. Behind the figure, architectural elements suggest a church or cathedral with a tower and tiled roof. The glass is richly colored in deep blues, purples, reds, and golds. Text appears along the bottom border of the panel.
theological_description: This stained-glass angel from Saint Brendan's Cathedral in Loughrea holds a lance, likely representing the Holy Lance (Lancea Sacra) that pierced Christ's side during the crucifixion. John records that 'one of the soldiers pierced his side with a spear, and at once there came out blood and water' (John 19:34). The blood and water were understood as symbols of the Eucharist and Baptism — the sacramental life of the Church flowing from Christ's wounded side. Angels bearing instruments of the Passion are a common motif in Christian art, reminding the faithful that these earthly objects of suffering have been taken up into heavenly glory. The lance fulfilled the prophecy of Zechariah: 'They shall look on him whom they have pierced' (Zech 12:10, cited in John 19:37). The church buildings behind the angel suggest the institution that was born from the wound the lance made.
